data.title: YZi Labs has invested tens of millions of dollars in Genius, but it still lacks followers.
data.description:
Author: Chloe, ChainCatcher

On April 13th, Genius, the Perp DEX trading terminal supported by YZi Labs, disclosed information about its first quarter airdrop event, distributing a total of 70 million GENIUS tokens, and simultaneously launching on Binance Alpha. However, this launch, which should have been a celebratory moment, triggered a crisis of trust within the community, stemming from the huge gap between official rules and community expectations.

CZ's endorsement, billions in trading volume, are profit-seekers fully committed?

Genius is an on-chain perpetual contract trading terminal with tens of millions of dollars invested by YZi Labs and CZ personally serving as an advisor, and it has been surrounded by a halo from the beginning.

Its platform aggregates over 10 public chains and more than 150 DEXs, focusing on CEX-level execution speed and on-chain privacy transactions. In January of this year, it achieved a weekly trading volume exceeding $2 billion, with a cumulative total trading volume exceeding $15 billion. According to crypto asset data platform RootData, Genius completed a $6 million seed round of financing in October 2024, led by CMCC Global, with participation from AVA Labs, Arca, Flow Traders, and other institutions. Furthermore, according to Genius co-founder Armaan Kalsi, YZi Labs joined the funding round this year, investing "tens of millions of dollars" in Genius Trading, and CZ also joined the company as an advisor. According to previous official announcements, 21% of the total supply of Genius tokens was allocated to community airdrops, to be carried out in three seasons, with 7% allocated in each season. Users could accumulate Genius Points (GP) through spot trading, with points directly corresponding to token allocations. The platform did indeed reward the market with real money during the distribution process, accumulating over $7 million in cash returns and $1.3 million in referral rewards, distributed weekly in USDC. This mechanism operated smoothly in the early stages, and the expectations for many arbitrageurs were quite clear. However, when the rules were suddenly rewritten on the eve of TGE, these investments instantly became sunk costs.

70% of the allocated user quota was burned, simply because the official team wanted to filter long-term holders

Season 1 officially ended on April 12th at 22:00 EST. The official announcement simultaneously revealed the airdrop details: a total of 70 million GENIUS tokens were allocated, but there were only two ways to claim them: 1) Claim immediately, which users could do within 7 days after TGE, but the system automatically burned 70% of the allocated quota, leaving users with only 30%; 2) Do not claim and wait one year, at which time the tokens would be locked in a smart contract, and 100% could be claimed after one year. The official narrative was "to filter long-term holders and reduce initial selling pressure."

The community, of course, was not buying it.

For active users who have already contributed a significant amount of transaction fees, these two options are essentially a dilemma: either accept the reality of a 30% discount on the tokens, or gamble a year's worth of time on a project with an uncertain future. Unsurprisingly, a large number of criticisms such as "anti-freeze," "scam," and "backstabbing" quickly emerged on X, with related tweets garnering tens of thousands of views within hours, and community discussions increasing nearly fourfold overnight. According to community users' estimates, Genius's opening FDV needs to reach at least $800 million for recent entrants to possibly break even. For a project that has been online for several months and still has some incomplete features, this threshold is clearly too high. "Genius is the last straw for freezer traders recently." "Refund?" Users are also unable to receive their full transaction fees. Faced with strong backlash from the community, the Genius team responded in less than 24 hours. In the early hours of April 13th, the official team issued announcements on X and Telegram, stating that they had "listened to community feedback" and added a third option to the airdrop file: a refund. Users are offered the option to burn 100% of their token allocation within 48 hours of the TGE, in exchange for their net transaction fees generated on the Genius platform. The refund will arrive within 48 hours of the application. However, there are still limitations to the "fee refund," and the final amount a user receives may differ from the fees they actually paid. Genius's refund calculation involves two filters. First, the platform has given back over $8.3 million to the community in the past few months through cash rebates and referral rewards. These previously received earnings will be deducted from the refund—meaning the more transactions and rebates received, the less will be refunded. For highly active heavy users, the net amount may be significantly reduced. Second, not all on-chain transaction fees go to Genius. The fees for each transaction are actually split among multiple stages, including the underlying DEX protocol (such as PancakeSwap). Genius, as an aggregation layer, only takes a small portion, and the refund is strictly limited to the portion credited to Genius's own account. In response, the official stance is clear: they will directly refute any accusations of "not fully refunding" the fees, arguing that those fees never belonged to Genius in the first place. Furthermore, the team has maintained an appeal channel for users with doubts about the refund amount; within the first week after TGE, users can submit objections through a dedicated form. The introduction of the Refund option has indeed garnered some positive publicity for the Genius team. Some users believe that in the crypto industry, a project team's willingness to listen to feedback and update rules within 24 hours demonstrates a commitment to long-term sustainability, at least providing a clear exit strategy for users who no longer wish to hold the tokens. However, critics point out that the so-called refund only covers the 5 basis points charged by the platform itself, not the full transaction fee, which is woefully inadequate for a large number of users engaging in fraudulent trading. Some users believe that "long-termism is practically written into the code," and this entire operation—from raising expectations to changing the rules at the last minute—is just another scam; no matter how the refund option remedies the situation, the time and transaction costs already invested cannot be fully recovered. Even on Polymarket, some users have begun betting that Genius's FDV after launch will be less than $200 million, reflecting the market's loss of confidence in this TGE operation. The rules are flawed; long-term holders cannot be cultivated through points incentives alone. Leng, a crypto KOL specializing in project research and analysis, criticized Genius, stating that this approach is no different from fraud: "They could choose to give only 2% initially instead of 7% and then burn 70% after receiving 30%." How can users support this? The project team hopes to attract early users to contribute liquidity and trading volume through the points system, but during the token distribution phase, they want to prevent these users from immediately selling. There is a fundamental contradiction between these two goals: many users' core motivation is to quickly realize their rewards, while the long-term holders the project team envisions cannot be cultivated through points incentives alone. When an airdrop's claiming rules require a high-percentage burning mechanism to filter users, it likely indicates a fundamental mismatch in the mechanism's design. The user group it attracts and the user group it ultimately aims to retain are not the same people from the start.

data.title: Is X Planning A Crypto Move Inside X Money? Product Chief’s Comment Triggers Speculation On Future Digital Asset Integration
data.description:
Is X Preparing to Rescue the Crypto Market

Speculation is reaching a fever pitch across the digital asset space following a brief social media post from Nikita Bier, the head of product at X.

While the crypto market has struggled to find its footing throughout 2026, Bier’s recent comments suggest that the platform formerly known as Twitter might be preparing a major intervention.

On 14 April 2026, Bier posted a short message that immediately went viral, stating,

“Crypto has had a rough year. Maybe we should launch something to fix it.”

The remark, though vague, has been interpreted by many as a signal that X is finally ready to merge its massive social reach with native blockchain infrastructure.

Crypto has had a rough year. Maybe we should launch something to fix it.

— Nikita Bier (@nikitabier) April 14, 2026

What Will X Money Look Like At Launch

The timing of Bier’s comment aligns closely with the anticipated rollout of X Money, a financial services suite championed by Elon Musk.

Musk confirmed last month that the service is scheduled to go live in April 2026 across more than 40 U.S. states.

𝕏 Money early public access will launch next month

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) March 10, 2026

The initial stack is substantial, featuring peer-to-peer transfers, bank deposits, and a Visa-backed debit card offering cashback rewards.

While the public documentation describes a fiat-heavy product, the platform has notably avoided ruling out the use of blockchain rails.

Some analysts believe X might use crypto infrastructure behind the scenes to handle instant settlements and offer the high yields Musk has teased.

Will Digital Assets Be Integrated Into The Everything App

While the initial version of X Money appears focused on traditional banking, the technical DNA of the product team suggests a deeper crypto connection.

X recently recruited Benji Taylor, the former chief product officer at Aave and head of design at Base, to help lead the project.

Bier noted that he had tracked Taylor’s work for years and pushed for his hiring.

Ladies and gentlemen, I'd like to welcome @benjitaylor to 𝕏, our new design lead.

I met Benji six years ago when I invested in his app: it was one of the most well-designed products I'd encountered. I knew right away he was on track to become one of the best designers in the… https://t.co/TFTZGCIm29

— Nikita Bier (@nikitabier) March 25, 2026

Furthermore, beta testers have reported seeing features like "smart cashtags," which could allow users to view real-time charts and trade assets directly from their timeline.

Whether this includes dogecoin or bitcoin at launch remains a subject of intense debate among investors.

Can X Solve The Industry Bot Problem

Beyond new features, Bier has been vocal about the technical hurdles facing the industry, particularly the prevalence of automated manipulation.

He recently claimed that "80% of crypto is just bots," and later remarked,

“I think when we removed the crypto bots, there were only 2000 people rugging each other back and forth, forever.”

I think when we removed the crypto bots, there were only 2000 people rugging each other back and forth, forever

— Nikita Bier (@nikitabier) April 13, 2026

To combat this, X has introduced automated systems to detect account compromises and verify ownership when large accounts suddenly promote unknown tokens.

This focus on security suggests that any financial product launched by X will prioritise a clean, verified environment to protect users from the phishing scams that have plagued the sector.

How Will 6% Yields Change The Payment Landscape

One of the most competitive aspects of the upcoming launch is the potential for interest-bearing accounts.

Reports from the closed beta suggest X Money could offer up to 6% annual yield on stored balances, a figure that rivals many stablecoin-based savings products.

🚨BREAKING: X MONEY Beta REVEALS 6% APY — 600 MILLION USERS Could Soon Earn Yield Inside the App 🤯🔥

Early beta testers confirm that @XMoney is offering a straight 6% ANNUAL YIELD on cash balances held inside the app. 👀

That’s HIGHER than most U.S. high-yield savings accounts… https://t.co/Xi5EF6MBp5pic.twitter.com/nyhH6yNXdO

— Diana (@InvestWithD) March 2, 2026

By providing FDIC-insured deposits alongside high returns, X is positioning itself to compete not just with other social media platforms, but with traditional banks and crypto exchanges alike.

If the platform successfully bridges the gap between social engagement and financial utility, it could provide the mainstream momentum that the crypto market has lacked throughout a difficult 2026.

data.title: Robinhood's Transformation Path in Wealth Management
data.description:
Author: @BlazingKevin_, Blockbooster Researcher

Robinhood ushered in a turning point in its business model in 2025. By developing its wealth management business, including launching IRA retirement accounts with disruptive rates, introducing high-yield cash products, and offering a full range of Robinhood Banking services, Robinhood successfully guided its younger user base away from high-frequency options and cryptocurrency trading towards long-term savings and investment.

Robinhood's financial data in 2025 fully validated the success of this strategy: full-year net revenue reached a record $4.5 billion, a year-over-year increase of 52%; full-year net profit reached $1.9 billion, a year-over-year increase of 35%; assets under custody (AUC) in retirement accounts reached $26.5 billion at the end of the fourth quarter, a surge of 102% year-over-year; total assets on the platform reached $324 billion, a year-over-year increase of 68%; and full-year net deposits reached $68 billion.

This article will analyze Robinhood's wealth management business development trajectory in 2025 using data, and explore its strategic advantages around five core dimensions: customer acquisition and asset transfer mechanisms, profit model evolution, ecosystem closed loop, brand reshaping, and operating cost structure. 1. Customer Acquisition and Asset Transfer Mechanisms Traditional wealth management typically relies on financial advisors for high-cost customer acquisition and relationship maintenance. Robinhood, however, adopts a highly internet-based subsidy and incentive mechanism to break down barriers to asset transfer, thereby achieving rapid growth in asset management scale.

1.1 Matching Bonus: The Economics of "Paying to Buy AUM"

Traditional retirement account providers (such as Fidelity and Charles Schwab) typically rely on brand reputation and investment advisory services to attract customers, while Robinhood has adopted a more direct and aggressive strategy: cash matching bonuses. Using real cash rewards to reduce the psychological cost of user migration.

For Robinhood Gold members, the platform offers up to 3% IRA deposit matching (1% for non-members).

For Robinhood Gold members, the platform offers up to 3% IRA deposit matching (1% for non-members).

Based on the 2025 IRS contribution cap of $7,000 for those under 50, users could receive up to $210 in free matching funds annually. More aggressively, Robinhood also offers a matching bonus of up to 3% for 401(k) or IRA assets rolled over from other brokerages. This means a user transferring $100,000 in 401(k) assets to a Robinhood IRA could immediately receive a $3,000 cash bonus. Is this economically worthwhile? We can break it down from the perspective of Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) and Customer Lifetime Value (LTV). By the end of 2025, customers had accumulated over $500 million in matching funds for retirement account transfers and contributions. Robinhood considers this expenditure as a customer acquisition cost. Because retirement accounts have extremely high stickiness (typically held for decades), this asset not only generates long-term net interest income (NIM) and potential investment advisory fees, but also locks users into becoming Gold members (annual fee of $50). Compared to traditional brokerages' customer acquisition costs of hundreds of dollars and high churn rates, Robinhood uses a 3% subsidy to acquire decades of highly sticky assets, with its LTV far exceeding CAC. 1.2 Frictionless Account Transfer Experience: Technological Democracy Subsidies alone are not enough; if the transfer process is cumbersome, users will still be deterred. Robinhood uses technology to significantly lower the barrier for users to transfer from traditional brokerages. By integrating Automated Account Transfer Services (ACATS), Robinhood enables seamless asset transfers across brokerages. Users simply enter their original brokerage account information within the app; Robinhood's clearing system automatically transfers assets in the background without requiring manual liquidation of existing assets. Robinhood also reimburses transfer fees charged by some brokerages (typically $75) under certain conditions. This "one-click migration" experience completely breaks down the asset transfer barriers traditionally built by institutions through cumbersome processes. 2. Evolution of the Profit Model from PFOF to Recurring Revenue In the past, Wall Street's criticism of Robinhood mainly focused on its heavy reliance on Payments for Order Flow (PFOF) and high-frequency trading. This model is extremely effective at generating revenue in bull markets but is extremely vulnerable in bear markets. In 2025, Robinhood successfully evolved towards a more stable asset management profit model. 2.1 Net Interest Income (NIM): Attracting Deposits with High-Yield Cash By offering highly competitive cash yields, Robinhood liberated "cash management" from the low-yield trap of traditional banks, thereby attracting massive amounts of deposits. At the end of 2023, the uninvested cash APY of Robinhood Gold members reached as high as 5.0%, far exceeding the national average interest rate for savings accounts at the time. As the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates, the APY gradually adjusts (to 3.35% by early 2026), but it remains significantly higher than the demand deposit rates of large banks. In the fourth quarter of 2025, Robinhood's Cash Sweep balance increased by 26% year-over-year, reaching $32.8 billion. This substantial amount of interest-earning assets brings Robinhood considerable net interest income. In the fourth quarter of 2025, its net interest income increased by 39% year-over-year to $411 million, primarily driven by growth in interest-earning assets and securities lending activities. Under specific interest rate cycles, this "earning the spread" model provides a very strong profit base.

2.2 Subscription Economy (Robinhood Gold): The SaaSification of Financial Services

Robinhood Gold's subscription service ($5 per month or $50 per year) is at the heart of its evolving profit model. It trades a monthly fee for high interest rates, in-depth investment research reports, 3% IRA matching funds, and 3% cashback on all credit cards. Essentially, this is an attempt to SaaS-ify financial services.

As of the end of Q4 2025, Robinhood Gold had a record 4.2 million subscribers, a 58% year-over-year increase, representing a penetration rate of over 15% among its 27 million financial customers.

This subscription model has significantly increased user engagement and average revenue per user (ARPU). Fourth-quarter ARPU increased by 16% year-over-year to $191, while third-quarter ARPU surged by 82% year-over-year. The table below illustrates the evolution of Robinhood's revenue structure: 3. Ecosystem Closed Loop Young users dislike downloading multiple apps to manage their finances. Robinhood understands this deeply and is building a super app encompassing investment, savings, consumption, and lending.

3.1 One-stop experience: Seamless integration of high- and low-frequency scenarios

In 2025, Robinhood launched or upgraded several products, forming a complete ecosystem loop:

  • High-frequency scenarios: zero-commission stock/options trading, 24/5 trading market, cryptocurrency trading.

  • Low-frequency defensive scenarios: IRA retirement accounts (3% matching), Robinhood Strategies (Gold members $250/year capped management fee).

  • Daily consumption scenarios: Robinhood Gold Credit Card (3% cashback), Robinhood Banking (4% APY savings, FDIC insurance).

Seamlessly integrating high-frequency speculative trading with low-frequency retirement investing and daily consumption within the same app is Robinhood's killer feature.

3.2 Traffic Conversion: Smooth Cross-Selling from "IPOs" to "Retirement"

Robinhood possesses the massive traffic that traditional asset management institutions dream of (27 million accounts as of the end of 2025). Its core strategy is to leverage high-frequency trading (such as Meme stocks and cryptocurrencies) and high-yield cash as traffic entry points, and then smoothly cross-sell low-frequency but high-value wealth management products.

For example, when a 22-year-old Gen Z user downloads Robinhood because they want to trade Dogecoin, they will be attracted by the 5% cash yield and become a Gold member. Subsequently, the app will use targeted push notifications to inform them that "as a Gold member, you can get 3% free matching funds when you activate IRA." When their assets accumulate to $100,000, the system will recommend "a robo-advisory service for only $250 per year." This conversion path from "traffic funnel" to "asset accumulation" allows Robinhood to acquire high-net-worth clients at extremely low marginal costs. 4. Building Trust 4.1 Compliance and Security Endorsement: Leveraging the Safety Net of Traditional Finance To reassure users that they can entrust their decades-long retirement savings to Robinhood, the company cleverly utilizes the safety net of traditional finance to endorse its innovative products. SIPC Protection and Additional Insurance: Robinhood emphasizes its status as a SIPC member (providing $500,000 in basic protection) and has purchased additional commercial insurance, offering each customer up to $50 million in securities protection and $1.9 million in cash protection. FDIC Deposit Insurance: When promoting Cash Sweep and Robinhood Banking, the platform partnered with multiple banks to provide users with up to $2.5 million in FDIC deposit insurance, far exceeding the standard coverage of $250,000 offered by ordinary banks. This narrative strategy of "safer than traditional banks" greatly alleviated young users' distrust of emerging Fintech platforms. 5. Operating Cost Structure Robinhood's ability to offer 3% IRA matching and a $250 capped investment advisory fee stems not only from its confidence in LTV but also from its significantly higher labor efficiency and underlying technology costs compared to traditional institutions. 5.1 Technology-Driven Automation and High Human Efficiency Robinhood doesn't have a large network of physical branches or a team of financial advisors. All its investment advisory services (such as ETF portfolios recommended by Robinhood Strategies) are highly automated, relying on algorithmic models for asset allocation and rebalancing. This "asset-light" model results in remarkable human efficiency. According to public data, as of the end of 2025, Robinhood had approximately 2,900 employees. With annual revenue of $4.5 billion, its revenue per employee reached $1.55 million. In contrast, traditional financial giants with tens of thousands of employees often have revenue per employee that is only half or even lower. 5.2 Diminishing Marginal Costs: The Power of a Self-Developed Clearing System Robinhood eliminated its reliance on third-party clearing institutions (such as Apex Clearing) as early as 2018, establishing its own self-developed clearing system. This infrastructure investment demonstrated significant operating leverage in 2025, a year of explosive growth in asset size. When AUC surged from $193 billion to $324 billion, the marginal cost of processing additional transactions and asset transfers was negligible due to the self-developed and highly automated clearing system. According to macro trend data, Robinhood's operating expenses for the full year of 2025 were $2.379 billion; despite a 52% surge in revenue, the growth in operating expenses was relatively manageable, directly driving full-year GAAP net profit to $1.9 billion and a significant increase in adjusted EBITDA margin. 6. Shifting User Profiles The core driver of Robinhood's wealth management success is the profound shift in the investment behavior of its younger user base. 6.1 A Younger User Base: A Structural Advantage According to ARK Invest research, Gen Z and Millennials make up 63% of Robinhood's user base, compared to only 14% at Charles Schwab and relatively limited at Vanguard. Robinhood's median user age is approximately 32 to 35 (2025 data), while the average age of clients at traditional brokerages like Schwab is over 50. This structural advantage is also reflected in the platform's asset density. Currently, Schwab's average assets under custody (AUC) per customer is approximately $250,000, significantly higher than Robinhood's current level of approximately $12,000. However, this gap is essentially an age gap. As Robinhood's younger user base gradually enters its peak wealth accumulation period, this gap will gradually narrow. 6.2 From "Meme Stocks" to "Long-Termism": A Profound Shift in Investment Behavior Robinhood CEO Vlad Tenev pointed out at the end of 2025 that a trend is underway: 19-year-old Gen Z individuals are actively opening retirement accounts. This trend is supported by data. According to the latest savings data cited by USA Today, the retirement savings rate for Gen Z has been rising for several years, reaching 6.2% in 2025, up from 5.9% in 2024, while savings rates for all other age groups are declining. Fidelity data shows that Gen Z investors allocate up to 95% of their IRA contributions to Roth accounts, demonstrating a clear understanding of long-term tax optimization. 6.3 Facilitating "The Largest Wealth Transfer in Human History" In the coming decades, it is estimated that approximately $124 trillion in assets will be transferred from Baby Boomers to Millennials and Gen Z. Given Robinhood's overwhelming market share among young people, when these young users inherit wealth, they are highly likely to choose to keep their funds within the familiar and better-feeling Robinhood ecosystem, rather than transferring them to traditional brokerages used by their parents. Conclusion: The Rise of a Financial Super App 2025 will be a watershed year in Robinhood's development history. It has successfully shed its label as a "casino for retail investors only," transforming into a comprehensive, mature, and highly competitive "super financial application." The core logic behind this transformation lies in Robinhood's deep understanding of the lifetime value of its user base. A 22-year-old Gen Z user might simply use Robinhood to buy a few ETFs today; tomorrow, they might open an IRA account and enjoy a 3% matching reward; the year after, they might roll their 401(k) from work into Robinhood; a few years later, when their assets grow to $100,000, they might subscribe to Robinhood Strategies for capped-fee professional investment advisory services; and finally, when they inherit money from their parents, they will naturally deposit it into Robinhood Banking. Through aggressive customer acquisition mechanisms, a stable recurring revenue model, a one-stop ecosystem, a rebuilt brand built on trust, and an extremely low-cost structure, Robinhood has perfectly prepared the infrastructure to handle this "largest intergenerational wealth transfer in human history."

data.title: Kraken Says Criminal Group Threatens To Release Internal Videos But Confirms Only Limited Client Data Exposure
data.description:
Extortion Threat Hits Crypto Exchange Security Team

Crypto exchange Kraken has confirmed it is facing an extortion attempt from a criminal group claiming access to internal material and client-related data, according to its chief security officer.

The firm says the attackers are threatening to release videos allegedly showing internal systems unless their demands are met.

However, Kraken has pushed back strongly, stressing that no core infrastructure was breached and client funds were never exposed.

Chief security officer Nick Percoco said the company will not give in to pressure from the group.

“We are currently being extorted by a criminal group threatening to release videos of our internal systems with client data shown if we do not comply with their demands.

It’s important to start with the most important points: our systems were never breached; funds were never at risk; we will not pay these criminals; we will not ever negotiate with bad actors.”

Kraken Security Update

We are currently being extorted by a criminal group threatening to release videos of our internal systems with client data shown if we do not comply with their demands. It’s important to start with the most important points: our systems were never…

— Nick Percoco (@c7five) April 13, 2026

Was Client Data Exposed Or Only Limited Access Viewed

Kraken said the impact was highly restricted, with only around 2,000 client accounts potentially viewed across two separate incidents.

That represents roughly 0.02% of its overall user base.

The exchange confirmed that while some client information may have been accessed, exposure was limited and affected users were notified.

A spokesperson for the company said it operates as Payward Inc. and declined to provide further detail on the exact data involved.

How The Security Incidents Unfolded Inside Support Systems

The first incident dates back to February 2025, when Kraken received intelligence about a video circulating on a criminal forum that appeared to show access to its customer support systems.

An internal investigation identified a support staff member linked to the activity.

Their access was immediately revoked, and affected customers were informed.

A second, similar case was later detected and handled in the same way, with the company tightening internal controls after each discovery.

Kraken says it’s being extorted right now… and they went public about it.

> Feb 2025 - one of their own support staff got caught accessing client accounts they had zero business touching. A Video of it was already floating on a criminal forum.

Employee fired, access cut,… https://t.co/ElmZFW82uepic.twitter.com/mLG7WT33Ch

— hitman42.eth (@ihitman42) April 14, 2026

Following these incidents, the group allegedly began issuing extortion demands, threatening to publish the material on media platforms and social networks.

Why Are Crypto Customer Support Systems Being Targeted

Security researchers say the focus on human access points is becoming more common across the crypto sector, as technical systems grow harder to breach directly.

Ari Redbord of TRM Labs said,

“What we are seeing is a shift toward the human layer. As technical defenses get stronger, the focus moves to the people inside the system, especially those whose job is to be accessible and helpful.”

Similar incidents have been seen elsewhere in the industry.

Coinbase previously faced attempts where customer support staff were bribed to extract user data, followed by ransom demands.

1) The attackers bribe overseas Coinbase support agents for customer data.

2) They gain access to some Coinbase user's names/addresses etc (no crypto wallets) which can enable social engineering scams.

3) The attackers demand payment of a $20M USD ransom or they will release… https://t.co/uQbUdJUJtF

— wale.moca 🐳 (@waleswoosh) May 15, 2025

Industry observers also point to the growing sophistication of attacks affecting decentralised finance systems, including a major exploit involving Drift (DeFi protocol), where nearly $300 million in assets was drained.

Is The Crypto Sector Facing Rising Insider-Driven Attacks

The case highlights a broader concern in digital asset markets, where criminals increasingly target employees rather than infrastructure vulnerabilities.

Blockchain analytics firm Chainalysis has noted rising physical and social engineering threats against crypto holders and staff, alongside so-called “wrench attacks” targeting individuals directly.

3/ Geographic Spread:
- US, Germany, Russia, Canada, Japan: Highest victim concentrations
- Eastern Europe, MENA, CSAO: most rapid H1 2024 to H1 2025 growth in victim totals pic.twitter.com/HoySF4mniF

— Chainalysis (@chainalysis) July 17, 2025

Kraken said it believes there is sufficient evidence to identify and potentially arrest those involved, adding that it is working with law enforcement agencies across multiple jurisdictions.

The company also said it is actively monitoring broader recruitment attempts targeting staff across crypto, gaming and telecom sectors, as part of what it describes as an evolving insider risk landscape.

data.title: A retrospective of 47 encryption incidents: all fell victim to the same human-caused vulnerability.
data.description:
Written by: Vladimir S.

Translated by: Saoirse, Foresight News

Only by treating security as a continuous, quantifiable cost, rather than a one-time check-in task, can one survive.

In the first three months of 2026, I did something most people in the crypto community were unwilling to do: I read through all the post-incident debriefings, on-chain forensic reports, and leaked Discord chat logs from all the major security incidents this year. A total of 47 incidents, with over $3.8 billion in assets disappearing. But none of these cases were so-called "genius zero-day smart contract vulnerability attacks."

Every time, the funds were transferred out through the front door—because someone deliberately opened the door.

Everyone's blaming the code, but I'm blaming people. Below is irrefutable evidence from 47 cases… I've categorized them by type of breach, and the patterns are clear at a glance. No fluff, just the brutal truth:

Overview of the remaining 32 incidents

  • Social engineering attacks (13 new incidents): Approximately 6.2

  • Coinbase Customer Service Outsourcing Bribery (May 2025, estimated impact $400 million): Overseas insiders carelessly leaked user account data.

  • 783 Bitcoin Whale Phishing (August 2025, $91 million): Victims thought they were communicating with the "official customer service" of their hardware wallets and handed over their mnemonic phrases in encrypted chat.

    • Developer and Supply Chain Intrusions (11 cases, losses exceeding $1.7 billion)

    Your so-called "secure" wallet infrastructure's security depends solely on the laptop the developer uses at 2 AM.

    • Bybit Cold Wallet Stolen (February 2025, $1.5 billion): Lazarus Group compromised a SafeWallet developer's device and used it to approve massive transfers. One device, one person, and the entire system collapsed.

    • Phemex Hot Wallet (January 2025, $85 million): Unauthorized access to the internal system was compromised after a targeted phishing attack.

    Private Key and Credentials Leaked (9 incidents, losses exceeding $650 million)

    It's 2026, and this is still happening. Really.

    • DMM Bitcoin (2024 legacy forensics case, $305 million): A classic private key breach case, still used by analysts as a template for exchange thefts in 2025–2026.

    DAO governance hijacking and flash loans (8 incidents, losses exceeding $220 million)

    Low voting rate + token-weighted voting = easy money with just a few million in liquidity.

    • GreenField DAO (2025, $31 million): Single-block flash loan governance attack.

    • UPCX Protocol (2025, $70 million): A typical proposal takeover attack.

    The remaining cases involve smaller sums, but the modus operandi is exactly the same: buy tickets at a low price, empty the coffers, and disappear.

    Without exception, all victims made the same fatal mistake: they treated human approval as a reliable security boundary.

    In every case, the decision of one person (or a small group of people) is the final checkpoint. There is no mandatory technical verification, no mandatory transaction simulation, no real-time identity verification, and no time delay. There is only one sentence: "Trust me, I am the signer."

    This is the only single point of failure. It's not the code, it's the people in the process.

    Seven-Layer Operational Security System

    I am not merely advocating this solution in theory, but have actually applied it to the security of two eight-figure-scale fund pools. The following complete system is sufficient to prevent any of the 47 security incidents mentioned above:

    1. Air-Isolated Devices + MPC Multi-Party Signature

    Never store mnemonic phrases on hot devices. Use hardware wallets or multi-party computation wallets to ensure that no one can see the complete key alone.

    Air-isolated signatures mean that the final signature is completed on hardware that has never been connected to the internet. MPC goes a step further: the key is never fully present in one place. Key fragments are distributed across multiple devices/multiple people, and a valid signature is only generated when a threshold is reached.

    Hot operations use MPC, large fund transfers use air-isolated cold storage, and flow rate limits and whitelists are set within the MPC engine so that even if a shard is leaked, the system will still intercept it.

    2. Cross-regional distributed multi-signature + high threshold

    The minimum is a 3/5 model, with signers distributed across different continents, devices, and time zones. The vulnerable 2/3 setup, where "everyone knows each other," is rejected.

    Even if an attacker uses social engineering to obtain two signers, a third signer from a different time zone, possibly sleeping or offline, is still needed. Signers are rotated quarterly, and hardware keys are mandatory; never use 2/3.

    3. Mandatory transaction simulation and preview

    Every signature must be fully rehearsed in a sandbox to clearly demonstrate what will happen on the chain.

    ... Blind signatures are prohibited. If the simulation results do not perfectly match expectations, the transaction will be terminated immediately. 4. Real-time Identity Verification + Challenge Response Verification Large transactions must involve real-time video calls, combined with pre-shared secret codes or real-time display of on-chain random numbers. Deep forgeries are ineffective here. Arrange calls in advance, record and audit the process, using tools such as Signal. 5. Time Lock + Delayed Execution Transactions exceeding $500,000 are placed in a 48–72 hour time lock queue, publicly verifiable, and support emergency suspension. This provides the team with time to respond to anomalies, preventing large-scale flash loan governance attacks and hasty authorization theft. 6. Automated Anomaly Monitoring + Emergency Shutdown Switch Provides on-chain and off-chain alerts for abnormal signer behavior, IP changes, and transaction patterns. Freezes the entire vault with one click. Set up a transaction that only requires multi-signature to trigger, used to suspend the vault or initiate emergency recovery. 7. Quarterly Red Team Drills + Failure Switch Hire white-hat hackers to conduct social engineering tests on the team. If a core signer is out of contact for 30 days, assets are automatically transferred to the recovery multi-signature address. Red team drills can uncover human vulnerabilities missed in audits, and the failure switch can handle situations such as "founder absconding with the key."

    The Drift Protocol Hack: Why Military-Grade Operational Security Systems Are Essential

    In April 2026, the North Korean state-owned hacking group UNC4736 spent six months forging identities, creating fake job postings, and gradually gaining the trust of Drift multisignature administrators, ultimately stealing $285 million. They didn't break any code; they broke the "human trust layer" that all projects still rely on.

    This incident illustrates a reality: once a state-owned power targets your funds, civilian-grade security is completely insufficient. Projects must adopt true military-grade operational security, based on the zero-trust principle of "never trust, always verify," implemented through the three major security principles of confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

    Every critical operation must have an aviation-grade checklist: mandatory simulation, real-time verification, independent review, and adhering to the mindset of "assuming it has already been compromised."

    Failing to meet this standard is tantamount to waiting for the next national-level hacker to walk in through the front door. Why is it necessary for project teams to establish dedicated internal security officers? Projects that survive to 2026 don't just spend money on audits; they hire full-time internal security officers whose sole responsibility is internal operational security and incident response. This isn't a part-time developer, nor is it something the team happens to manage incidentally. This person must be familiar with all reliable auditing firms, on-chain funding teams, and white-hat emergency response groups, and know who to contact first in case of an incident. Traditional industry security officers are often former military, police, or special forces personnel because they have emergency muscle memory. The crypto industry is the same—it needs someone deeply versed in the on-chain world, capable of coordinating multi-signature freezes at 3 AM and simultaneously notifying security agencies. Without such a core person in charge, even the most perfect seven-layer system will collapse instantly under real pressure. The Resolv Labs incident: Detection alone is far from enough. The Resolv Labs retrospective, released in early April 2026, is a typical case of 11 out of 47 supply chain and infrastructure breaches. Attackers used GitHub privileges left behind by outsourced personnel to enter the system, laterally penetrate cloud facilities, tamper with signature policies, mint 80 million unauthorized tokens, and steal approximately $25 million in ETH. Although real-time monitoring had flagged the abnormal transactions, it took the team more than an hour to begin taking action. This is why a dedicated security manager is essential: they can instantly connect to the emergency response and forensics teams, and their emergency procedures are ingrained in their muscle memory. This is also why every project needs bug bounties, continuous audits, and audit competitions. Smart teams treat security investments like insurance: the returns aren't visible in normal times, but they're lifesavers when something goes wrong. The mentality of "we've already audited it once" is the root cause of billions lost across 47 projects. The following must be done: Maintain a public and well-funded bug bounty program (minimum $50,000–$250,000 based on TVL); Audit every update before it goes live; Hold at least one audit competition per quarter. A single major hacking incident can destroy a project. Only by treating security as a continuous, quantifiable cost, rather than a one-time task, can you survive. Personal Security Advice: Even the strongest seven-layer protocol protection cannot prevent your own computer or mobile phone from becoming a weak link. Windows/Linux devices: Install Malwarebytes + professional EDR endpoint protection; iPhone: Enable Lock Mode, install iVerify to detect spyware and zero-click vulnerabilities; Mac: S1 is recommended, FileVault must be enabled for new Apple chips, disable AirPlay if not used, and set a password for the screen saver. For all operations involving keys or signatures, use a dedicated "clean" MacBook: Never log into iCloud, avoid unauthorized internet access, reinstall the system quarterly, and keep it in a Faraday bag when not in use. Overlay enterprise-grade DLP data loss prevention and lightweight SIEM log alerts. Trail of Bits' OpSec minimal list can be used directly. Personal security is the last line of defense. Once an attacker gets close, there's no pause button. This system is not just theoretical. I've used it in stress tests against top-tier hacker attacks, proving its effectiveness. While smart contract security improves year by year, people's security awareness hasn't kept pace. In 2026, if you're still operating protocols, DAOs, or large personal wallets, and still relying on claims like "trustworthy team" or "audited contracts," your vigilance is far from sufficient. You could be the next victim.

data.title: CoinGecko: Spot CEX Report 2026
data.description:
Author: CoinGecko

Since the birth of the cryptocurrency market, centralized exchanges (CEXs) have played a crucial role in providing liquidity to retail and institutional users. In 2025 alone, the top 12 CEXs traded nearly $21 trillion in the spot market. As the industry matures, focus has shifted to the sustainability of spot trading activity and the health of the underlying reserves supporting this massive global trading volume.

The landscape of centralized exchanges is changing. While USDT and USDC dominate trading pairs, the listing performance of new tokens still faces challenges. With total global reserves reaching $225.4 billion in 2026, funds are flowing from institutional giants to highly liquid, retail-dominated platforms. The following is an analysis of the current market situation.

Four Highlights of CoinGecko's 2026 Spot Centralized Exchange Report

  1. USDT and USDC are the primary stablecoin underlying assets, accounting for 66.6% of all trading pairs on the top 12 centralized exchanges.

  2. Only about 32% of newly listed tokens on the top 12 centralized exchanges (CEXs) saw an immediate price increase after listing.

  3. Between 2024 and 2026, the value of the underlying assets of the top 12 central exchanges (CEXs) increased from $152.1 billion to $225.4 billion.

  4. Exchanges dominated by retail investors have higher reserve utilization rates compared to exchanges favored by institutional investors.

1. USDT and USDC are the main underlying assets of stablecoins, accounting for 66.6% of all trading pairs on the top 12 centralized exchanges.

The majority of spot trading volume on the top 12 central exchanges is completed through stablecoin trading pairs such as USDT and USDC.

Of the 9,870 stablecoin trading pairs across 12 centralized exchanges, 9,646 are USDT or USDC pairs, representing 97.7% of all stablecoin trading pairs. Meanwhile, there are 4,615 non-stablecoin trading pairs, accounting for 31.9% of the 14,485 trading pairs. However, the share of non-stablecoin trading pairs does not correlate with their trading volume share. At its peak in November 2024, non-stablecoin trading pairs accounted for only 23% of the market share. 2. Among the top 12 centralized exchanges (CEXs), only about 32% of newly listed tokens experienced an immediate price increase after listing.

Among the top 12 exchanges, Upbit's token price performance far surpasses others after listing, with 67% of newly listed tokens seeing price increases within 30 days. However, its listing rate is one of the lowest. Binance and OKX follow closely behind, both with a listing rate of 50%.

After listing on the top 12 exchanges, token price performance varies significantly in the short term (0-29 days). However, after 30 days, the price fluctuation range narrows significantly.

On average, only 25% of newly listed tokens remain up after 30-59 days. Looking at longer timescales, this percentage shows a near-linear decline across all exchanges. The only exception is Coinbase, where listed cryptocurrencies experience a second wave of growth six months after listing. After 12 months, less than 10% of tokens listed on most top exchanges are still priced above their initial listing price. Notably, tokens listed on Upbit, while having the best initial performance, also experienced the fastest decline, with all newly listed tokens falling below their initial price after 300-329 days. 3. Between 2024 and 2026, the underlying asset value of the top 12 Central Exchanges (CEXs) increased from $152.1 billion to $225.4 billion.

Despite rising prices for Bitcoin (BTC) and Ethereum (ETH) reserves on most exchanges, price volatility and increased regulation are prompting users to reallocate funds from major exchanges to other platforms.

The average value of the underlying assets of the top 12 centralized exchanges is projected to grow by 69.6% from $152.1 billion at the beginning of 2024 to $225.4 billion by the end of February 2026.

During this period, eight exchanges saw their net reserve value increase, with Binance leading the pack. Its reserves doubled over the past two years, from $46.7 billion to $93.4 billion. Meanwhile, Coinbase holds the largest Bitcoin reserves, exceeding 800,000 coins. Binance follows closely with 669,000 coins. Despite the increase in reserve value, it's worth noting that Coinbase experienced significant outflows of 20% and 41% from its Bitcoin and Ethereum reserves, respectively. Some of these outflows have been redeposited into smaller exchanges like Bitget and MEXC, which saw significant surges in reserves, increasing by +262.0% and +274.6%, respectively. 4. Compared to exchanges favored by institutional investors, exchanges dominated by retail investors have higher reserve utilization rates. While centralized exchanges hold large reserves of various crypto assets, the way user deposits are used varies significantly across different platforms. More regulated centralized exchanges like Coinbase, Binance, and Kraken have lower trading volume-to-reserve ratios, around 0.1. This may be because their customer base leans more towards institutional users, who rely more on these platforms for custody than for trading. Meanwhile, exchanges like Bybit and Bitget have high trading volumes and deposits, with average ratios of 0.3 and 0.5 respectively from January 2024 to February 2026. On the other hand, centralized exchanges with smaller reserves, such as MEXC, HTX, and KuCoin, have wider asset turnover ratios, ranging from 1.44 to 2.04, indicating that most users' trading volume far exceeds these exchanges' reserves.

data.title: Peter Magyar Has Become The Next Hungarian Prime Minister, As The Country Prepares For A Complete Crypto Revolution...
data.description:
A turning point for Hungary’s crypto direction?

Hungary’s political reset following the victory of Péter Magyar and his Tisza Party may open the door to a more crypto-friendly regulatory environment—but for now, any shift remains purely speculative.

This is the New prime minister of Hungary 🇭🇺 Peter Magyar thanking supporters before his victory speech for coming through for their country

Music 🎶 in the background:

to My Way by Frank Sinatra.💙💙#Hungarypic.twitter.com/zUFcvBOExP

— Shruti Dhore (@ShrutiDhore) April 13, 2026

The April 12 election marked the end of Viktor Orbán’s 16-year rule, replacing it with a pro-EU administration that could take a markedly different stance on digital asset regulation.

Under Orbán, Hungary introduced one of the European Union’s most restrictive crypto frameworks. Amendments to the 2025 Crypto Act criminalized “unauthorized crypto exchange services,” with penalties of up to two years in prison, while also introducing a SARA certificate validation system for all domestic crypto-to-fiat and crypto-to-crypto transactions.

🇭🇺A new law in Hungary is driving cryptocurrency exchanges out of business.

Hungary has introduced a so-called "crypto-asset exchange validation service," imposing licensing requirements for businesses which "validate crypto-asset exchange transactions" under threat of criminal… pic.twitter.com/SI3grl7WV0

— The Rage (@theragetech) March 21, 2026

The result was a tightly controlled environment that many in the industry viewed as a de facto gatekeeping mechanism—one that favored licensed domestic players while limiting access to international platforms.

Magyar’s pro-EU positioning raises the possibility that Hungary could move closer to the bloc’s unified regulatory framework under MiCA.

The European Commission has already launched infringement proceedings against Hungary, arguing that the SARA validation system conflicts with MiCA’s goal of regulatory harmonization across member states. A new government aligned with Brussels could, in theory, resolve this dispute more quickly—potentially removing the validation layer even before broader legislative reforms are introduced.

Brussels’ Welcome Gift: 27 Conditions and a Receipt for Your Sovereignty

What a charming introduction to office.

Hungary’s new prime minister doesn’t receive congratulations from Brussels—he receives a checklist. Twenty-seven conditions, neatly packaged, attached to €30… pic.twitter.com/Pvjt7jkIhc

— Vicky Richter🇩🇪🇺🇸🇧🇷🇬🇧 (@VickyRichterUSA) April 14, 2026

However, this remains a hypothetical pathway. No formal policy announcements, timelines, or legislative proposals have been confirmed.

What a rollback would actually require

Reversing Hungary’s crypto crackdown would not be a simple policy switch. It would likely involve a sequence of coordinated actions, including amending or repealing the criminal provisions that target unlicensed crypto services, dismantling the SARA validation framework that governs transaction approvals, and working alongside EU authorities to resolve the ongoing infringement proceedings.

Each of these steps involves separate legal and institutional processes, meaning that even under a supportive government, change would take time—and remains far from guaranteed.

Hungary’s strict regulatory stance has already had tangible effects on the market. Revolut, which serves more than 2 million users in the country, suspended crypto-related services—including buying, staking, and deposits—following the implementation of the 2025 rules.

Hungary has enacted strict new cryptocurrency laws effective July 1, criminalizing “unauthorized” crypto trading with penalties of up to 5 years in prison for individuals and 8 years for service providers. Revolut has suspended crypto services in Hungary. The sweeping law,…

— Wu Blockchain (@WuBlockchain) July 14, 2025

A regulatory softening could, in theory, pave the way for such platforms to re-enter the market. But again, this depends entirely on whether policy changes materialize.

Hungary’s situation is increasingly being viewed as a test case for how far individual EU member states can diverge from MiCA’s standardized framework.

If the new government chooses to align more closely with EU rules, it could signal a shift toward greater regulatory consistency across the bloc. On the other hand, maintaining or only partially revising existing measures would suggest that national-level deviations remain possible—even under MiCA.

A shift in direction, not yet in policy

For now, the key takeaway is distinction: Hungary has experienced a political shift, not yet a regulatory one.

🇪🇺 The European Commission sent formal notices to 12 countries for incomplete crypto tax transparency implementation, targeting #Bitcoin info-sharing to curb evasion. Hungary faces separate #MiCA warning over national conflicts. pic.twitter.com/j4xkd9m8w2

— The Bitcoin Act (@The_Bitcoin_Act) February 3, 2026

While the change in leadership introduces the possibility of a more crypto-friendly environment, the actual outcome will depend on concrete legislative action, regulatory coordination, and political priorities in the months ahead.

Until then, traders, platforms, and investors are left watching for early signals—aware that in crypto policy, direction can change quickly, but implementation rarely does.

data.title: The underlying logic of bottleneck transmission in the AI ​​computing power industry chain
data.description:
Author: qinbafrank; Source: X,@qinbafrank

Recently, several investment banks, including Morgan Stanley, JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America, Goldman Sachs, UBS, Citigroup, Bernstein, and HSBC, have released updated reports related to AI/semiconductors/power/storage. The bottleneck in AI hardware has expanded from the single dimension of "GPU supply" to a collective shortage across five dimensions: power, chips, storage, equipment, and materials.

The scale of AI demand has exceeded all prediction ranges of traditional power planning, semiconductor equipment capacity, storage price models, and robot installation assumptions.

Morgan Stanley's global thematic research review points out that the weekly consumption of large language model tokens globally surged from 6.4 trillion to 22.7 trillion within three months, an increase of 2.5 times. The US data center power shortage for 2025-2028 is projected at 55 gigawatts. JPMorgan Chase's first coverage of high-performance computing project bonds for data centers directly indicates a 122 gigawatt financing gap over the next five years. The US five-year power plan has surged from 101 gigawatts to 230 gigawatts, with 44% of new projects waiting more than four years to connect to the grid. Bank of America's latest target price report for Alphabet directly revised its 2026 capital expenditures upwards to $181.5 billion, doubling year-on-year, while free cash flow decreased by 62% year-on-year. These three sets of data are not from the same framework, but rather independent profiles from three independent institutions using different research paths. The evolution of bottlenecks in the semiconductor industry chain (especially in the field of AI computing power) progresses in a clear sequence: “computing (GPU) → storage (HBM, etc.) → optical interconnect → power/liquid cooling.” This is the industry consensus for 2025-2026. As AI training/inference clusters expand from single racks (dozens of GPUs) to ultra-large scales (thousands to hundreds of thousands of GPUs), solving the bottleneck in one link will immediately expose the next physical/supply chain constraint, forming a “Leontief-style” complementary constraint (no shipment is possible without any one of them).

It's necessary to understand why this evolution has occurred, the current situation, and the underlying physical/engineering reasons:

1. First-stage bottleneck: GPU computing (dominantly driving growth 2022-2024) core limitations

Wafer capacity for high-end GPUs (such as NVIDIA Hopper H100 → Blackwell B200 → Rubin) + advanced packaging.

... Why is it a bottleneck? Large AI models require massive parallel computing, and TSMC's 4nm/3nm/2nm logic process + CoWoS (2.5D/3D packaging) capacity became the biggest bottleneck. Even if there were enough front-end wafers, the back-end's ability to stack and package logic chips + HBM couldn't keep up, preventing the production of the entire GPU. Mitigation: TSMC significantly expanded CoWoS (doubling capacity in 2024-2025), and NVIDIA Blackwell has already shipped in large quantities. However, this only unlocked the "computing" aspect; new problems immediately emerged. 2. Second-stage bottleneck: Storage (HBM high-bandwidth memory, becoming the most scarce in 2024-2025) Core limitation: HBM3/HBM3e/HBM4 capacity. Why is relay processing becoming a bottleneck? While GPU computing power has increased, the explosive growth of model parameters (trillions or even tens of trillions of parameters) has created a "memory bandwidth" barrier. HBM can transfer several terabytes of data per second, more than 20 times faster than conventional DDR memory. Because HBM is located adjacent to the logic chip, data doesn't need to be transmitted over long distances, thus saving energy. A single B200 GPU requires 192GB+ HBM3e, and the total HBM volume in a single rack (NVL72) has reached 30-40TB, with bandwidth requirements far exceeding traditional DRAM. Current supply chain situation: Only SK Hynix, Samsung, and Micron can mass-produce HBM. The process is complex (Through Silicon Vias + stacking), and all HBM was sold out by 2025. Demand remains high in 2026, with prices soaring by 246% year-on-year. Even if the GPU chip is ready, assembly and delivery are impossible without HBM, causing delays in the deployment of the entire AI cluster.

Result: Storage has transformed from a "commodity" into a strategic bottleneck, with storage accounting for up to 30% of capital expenditure.

3. Third-stage bottleneck: Optical interconnect (currently transitioning in 2025-2026)

Core limitation: The physical limitations of copper cables (NVLink/NVSwitch) in terms of bandwidth, distance, power consumption, and weight.

Why the inevitable shift to optical: Copper cables can still be used within a single rack (72 GPUs), but when expanding to multiple racks or even interconnecting thousands of GPUs, copper cables suffer from severe attenuation (effective distance <1 meter at 1.8TB/s bandwidth), explosive weight (over 5,000 copper cables in an NVL72 rack, totaling 1.36 tons), and high power consumption (pluggable optical modules replacing copper cables would consume an additional 20,000 watts). Signal integrity, latency, and heat dissipation cannot support larger clusters.

Solution: Shift to optical interconnects (CPO co-packaged optics + silicon photonics technology). The optical engine is directly packaged next to the GPU/ASIC, using fiber optics for scale-out, resulting in higher bandwidth density, lower power consumption per bit, and longer distance. NVIDIA is heavily investing in optics companies at GTC 2026, with a surge in demand for 800G/1.6T optical modules. Lite, Broadcom, Coherent, and Ayar Labs are emerging as new winners. NVIDIA is heavily investing in optics companies at GTC 2026, with a significant investment in optics companies. Current Progress: Copper cabling has reached its limit; optical interconnects are transitioning from "optional" to "mandatory," breaking through the performance ceiling of AI data centers.

4. Fourth Stage Bottleneck (Current Cutting Edge): Power + Liquid Cooling (Becoming the Ultimate Physical Constraint from 2026)

Core Limitations: Power Constraints + Thermal Constraints + Grid Connection. Why is it the Ultimate Bottleneck: Each GPU has increased from 300W to 700-1200W, and a single rack has surged from 10-20kW (CPU era) to 120-200kW+ or even higher. Traditional air cooling has a physical upper limit of only 20-50kW, and its noise, airflow, and energy consumption are unacceptable.

Power Side: Data centers require GW-level power supply; grid connection queues can last for years, and the delivery cycle for equipment such as transformers and solid-state transformers has been extended to 100 weeks. Microsoft's CEO once bluntly stated, "We have GPUs, but no power outlets."

Liquid cooling side: A switch to Direct-to-Chip (DTC) or immersion liquid cooling is necessary, combined with microfluidics, cold plates, and other technologies. TSMC has already demonstrated silicon-based liquid cooling on its CoWoS platform, supporting >2.6kW TDP. Liquid cooling/thermal management vendors such as Vertiv (VRT) are becoming new core components of the infrastructure. Chain reaction: PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) requirements of <1.2, waste heat recovery, and grid connection of nuclear power/new energy sources have become new topics. Even if all the preceding aspects are resolved, without electricity and cooling, server racks cannot be installed and operated. The essential logic behind the bottleneck shift in the AI ​​computing power industry chain: AI computing power is not a "single point" problem, but a system-level Leontief production function—GPUs, HBMs, interconnects, power, and cooling must be matched according to the lowest weakest link. Each time a hyperscaler (Google, Microsoft, Meta, etc.) solves one bottleneck, it immediately propels capital and innovation to the next stage. Currently (in 2026), we are in a transitional period of "accelerated deployment of optical interconnects + large-scale commercialization of power/liquid cooling." New bottlenecks may emerge in the future (such as lasers, fiber optic materials, or grid transformers), but the "computing → storage → optical → power/cooling" chain has become the industry's recognized path. This also explains why investment logic has shifted from NVIDIA/TSMC to the HBM three giants (SK Hynix, etc.), optical manufacturers (Lumentum, Coherent), and liquid cooling/power infrastructure (Vertiv, related power supply companies). Each bottleneck shift reshapes the value distribution of the entire semiconductor + data center industry chain.

data.title: Three reasons why Bitcoin may be undervalued at present
data.description:
Source: Bitcoin Magazine Pro, compiled by Shaw Jinse Finance

Price Trend

Bitcoin traded in a narrow range around $69,000 at the beginning of the week, before surging to $72,700 after Trump announced a two-week ceasefire agreement between the US and Iran. This move triggered nearly $600 million in short covering.

However, Bitcoin failed to continue its upward momentum, and the breakdown of US-Iran negotiations led to a subsequent pullback to $70,785. This price is still up 2.22% from a week ago.

Figure 1: Price Movement Over the Past Week. Looking at the bigger picture, Bitcoin remains within the range we pointed out back in February. Earlier this week, Bitcoin failed to break out of this range, which is not a good sign for the bulls. However, if Bitcoin does manage to break out, the initial target will be the 200-day moving average, which is currently trending downwards at $87,500. Figure 2: BTC Rejected at the Top of the Price Range. Three Reasons Why Bitcoin May Be Undervalued. In last month's report, analyst Philip Swift outlined his views on Bitcoin's current cyclical position. Below, we extract some data from that analysis to explain why Bitcoin may currently be undervalued. Below are three reasons why Bitcoin may be currently undervalued: 1. The derivatives market is becoming healthier. [Image of a graph showing a funding rate in recent weeks, which is not directly related to the previous sentence.] This indicates that derivatives traders are positioning themselves, betting on further price declines. Historically, this phenomenon typically occurs near market bottoms. In simpler terms, a large amount of excess leveraged capital betting on price increases has been cleared from the market. This makes the market's fundamentals more stable. If this environment continues, it will reduce the risk of a sharp price drop and instead create conditions for a more sustainable rebound. For Bitcoin at present, this is undoubtedly a significant positive signal.

2. Institutions Continue to Accumulate Bitcoin

Figure 4: BTC Treasury Tracker

Saylor has been almost obsessively buying Bitcoin. The Bitcoin holdings of Strategy, a publicly traded company, continue to climb, leaving all other institutions far behind.

This constitutes a stable source of structural demand in the short term, with Strategy buying more Bitcoin in recent weeks than all ETFs combined. As long as Strategy's STRC product remains successful, Thaler is expected to continue accumulating more Bitcoin. We have every reason to expect Strategy to hold over 1 million Bitcoins sometime this year. Currently, the company holds 766,970 Bitcoins.

3. Valuation is becoming extremely attractive

Figure 5: BTC Investor Tool (2-Year Moving Average Multiplier)

From a valuation perspective, Bitcoin is currently in a price range historically representing a strong long-term investment opportunity, with its price below the 2-year moving average (the green line in the chart above). This is a position that people will look back on with regret, saying, "I wish I had bought Bitcoin at this price."

While the current market environment may be fraught with uncertainty, data shows a more positive outlook is emerging. Leveraged funds have been fully liquidated. Structural demand remains strong. Valuation levels are at historically low levels. Overall, these factors suggest that Bitcoin's current market price may be undervalued compared to its long-term trajectory.

data.title: 11 people, $900 million in profits: The story of Hyperliquid and its founder Yan
data.description:
Title: Beyond the Sky; This article was written by Dom Cooke, Executive Editor of Colossus, after a week-long visit to Hyperliquid's Singapore office; Compiled by: Jinse Finance Claw

Summary: Jeffrey Yan rejected $100 million in investment, airdropped billions of dollars to strangers, and requires bodyguards when he travels. How did he build Hyperliquid, a blockchain and cryptocurrency trading platform, into the world's most profitable startup per capita?

It was a Friday in January, before dawn, when a 43-year-old man was kidnapped from his home in Saint-Léger-sous-Cholet, western France. He was driven 30 miles to a small commune in Basse-Goulaine, where he was beaten, tied up, and abandoned.

Twelve hours later, as the sun set over the Paris suburbs, three men armed with handguns kicked down the door of a family home in Verneuil-sur-Seine. They beat a couple in front of their children, tied up the four people in the house with zip ties, ransacked the entire house, and then headed for the train station. This was the 70th such attack globally in less than a year. Two days later, I boarded a flight to Singapore. I flew to visit an 11-person team, but the first person I met in the office wasn't one of them. He was a burly American with a buzz cut and stubble, sitting behind an Apple laptop on a small table in the corner of the lounge area; his physique suggested he wasn't there to code. He was a bodyguard. One of the company's co-founders, using the pseudonym "iliensinc" (an abbreviation of Aliens Incorporated), led me from the hotel to the office. Walking through streets like a canopy of rain trees, she told me they didn't always stay in this area of ​​Singapore. The business started in a co-working space in the financial district, but one of her co-founders—the only one on the team who didn't use an alias at work—began to attract attention. At first, it was just stares as people struggled to recognize his face. Then, strangers started approaching him. Then someone followed him into the apartment building's elevator. So, the company moved to a quieter location, a building no one would think to look for them in. Even their cleaning staff didn't know what they did. She assumed she was cleaning for a trading company that made plush cats. There were 34 plush toys in the office, so this misunderstanding was understandable. The company mascot was a cat named Hypurr, 12 of which perched on cabinets, in addition to sharks, lizards, koalas, penguins, and dragons, several of which hung on Dell monitors like fluffy gargoyles. An engineer was in charge of most of the "creatures." His wife wouldn't let him take any more toys home, so he brought them to the office. The team didn't correct the cleaning staff's assumptions. This is because Hyperliquid, a blockchain and cryptocurrency trading platform, is one of the most profitable companies per capita on Earth. Last year, its 11 employees generated over $900 million in profits. Founded just three years ago, it has a market capitalization of $10 billion and has never accepted a single dollar of venture capital. The man behind it, Jeffrey Yan, 31, has become one of the most prominent faces in this industry where "success often leads to being held hostage," not entirely by choice. Before Hyperliquid, Jeffrey Yan lived in Puerto Rico and almost single-handedly ran one of the largest anonymous trading firms in the crypto space. It was known as "Chameleon Trading"—"Chameleon" being his online nickname from a high school video game. He started with $10,000 he had saved and grew the company thousands of times annually in two and a half years. When he told me about his returns, he immediately tried to convince me not to think it was amazing. I took his objections to heart, but also the fact that "Chameleon" had made him incredibly wealthy. He was 27 then, and free. To every surfer, bartender, and waitress in San Juan, he was just another kid in beach shorts. Now, sitting cross-legged in a grey armchair in a heavily guarded office in Singapore, barefoot, wearing black shorts and a dark blue T-shirt, he explains to me why the entire financial system needs to be rebuilt from scratch. The question I wanted to know was why he would trade one lifestyle for the other. He says it wasn't for the money. Jeffrey Yan didn't grow up in a wealthy family, and his life doesn't suggest any interest in a life of luxury. He wears the same Lululemon shorts and T-shirt every day. He owns 15 pairs of those shorts and 10 t-shirts, each in three colors. There's no evidence of wealth in his office; the furniture is from previous tenants. The only additions to the team are two board games in the break room, NFTs on the wall, and a few plush cats. This was confirmed when I spotted four books on the shelf and recognized one as Frank Slootman's *Amp It Up*. This is a management book whose central argument is that most people don't work hard enough. I mentioned this to "iliensinc," and she shrugged. The motto is theirs, but the book isn't. The three bottles of Grey Goose vodka and Macallan whiskey I found in the kitchen weren't theirs either; they weren't at a community event two years ago, which didn't even meet the minimum spending requirement. This team only drinks tea. It's not out of love for cryptocurrencies either. As a barometer of the industry, Bitcoin has fallen about 30% from its early October peak. Gold, which Bitcoin was supposed to replace, has risen 7% in the same three months. Most tokens have performed worse. When I asked about the negative sentiment surrounding the industry, Jeffrey Yan didn't defend it. "There's a lot of questionable behavior in this space," he said. "People realizing these things aren't as good as they're advertised is probably healthy." He doesn't consider Hyperliquid a crypto company. “People don’t call us an internet company anymore,” he told me. “We use crypto, but that doesn’t define us.” Before Hyperliquid, only two people on the team, including Jeffrey Yan, had prior crypto experience. This was partly intentional. As he described, the early crypto community was primarily interested in making quick money. He said he was doing long-term planning, which aligned better with people who thought more like technologists than traders. But it was also a supply issue. Hyperliquid recruited talent from the podium of the International Mathematical and Science Olympiads. Jeffrey Yan won a gold medal in physics at 18. One of his engineers holds a silver medal in informatics, and another trained on the US national team. Jeffrey Yan hopes to hire more people like that, and he’s added two more since my visit earlier this year. But due to years of scams and broken promises, and the recent rise of artificial intelligence, the pool of people willing to build in the crypto space at this level has narrowed. So, what is Yan, who has already made enough money to live any kind of life, doing here? The answer, at least to the outside world, is becoming increasingly clear. Hyperliquid is a blockchain with its own trading platform. In traditional exchanges, the company holds your funds and controls the infrastructure. On Hyperliquid, you have custody of your own funds, and the platform is public. Yan's vision—stated without irony—is to accommodate all financial transactions. Whether this is ambition or absurd depends on whether you look at the plush cats or the platform's data. Because in the months following my visit, market trading methods that have been in use for over 100 years are beginning to shift in small, measurable ways. Hyperliquid started in 2023 with perpetual contracts, a type of derivative that is now the largest single market in the crypto space. Perpetual contracts (PERPs) are bets on the price of an asset you never own; unlike traditional futures, they never expire. The market size for such games is six to eight times larger than the spot market for the asset itself, at approximately $7 trillion per month. Until recently, almost all such trading was conducted through centralized exchanges, the largest being Binance. No decentralized platform had previously been able to challenge its position; Hyperliquid was the first, and its market share has grown to approximately 14% of Binance's. Then, in October 2025, Hyperliquid did something that centralized exchanges couldn't: it allowed anyone to open a new perpetual contract market on the platform for any asset with a price source. An independent organization called Trade[XYZ] is the most active. It started with the silver market. By January 2026, its 24-hour trading volume had reached approximately 2% of that of the CME (Chicago Mercantile Exchange); the CME, founded in 1898, is the world's largest derivatives exchange. Trade[XYZ] then launched crude oil contracts. Oil was traded in markets that were closed on weekends. On a Saturday in late February 2021, the US and Israel began bombing Iran. The CME closed, but Hyperliquid did not. Daily crude oil trading volume surged from $21 million to $3.7 billion. A month later, Trade[XYZ] launched perpetual contracts for the S&P 500 index and received official permission from S&P Dow Jones Indices. It traded 24/7, including weekends.

The most important product on Hyperliquid is now being developed by people who don't work for Yan and never will.

The founder of Trade[XYZ], who requested anonymity, bought his first Bitcoin for $66 in 2013 and spent the following years as an investor, not a builder. He hadn't originally planned to start a business. He told me that he wouldn't have stayed in the crypto industry if it weren't for Yan. "Hyperliquid has the potential to save cryptocurrency," he said. Despite this, it doesn't explain why Hyperliquid might become what Yan predicted (in an industry where things tend to look good until they go wrong), nor why he would give up his life in Puerto Rico. These questions lingered in my mind during my first afternoon in the office, as I chatted with "iliensinc" in the break room. A plush cat sat on the table between us, and the air still smelled of ginger and sesame from lunch. She told me that three years ago, when Yan announced the end of "Chameleon," the team had asked him the same question. Her answer didn't start with cryptocurrency, but with what kind of person Yan was. She said I should ask his mother. Yan liked to have meetings outdoors. We sat on a covered patio with four gray armchairs and a coffee table. Cars drove by on the street below. Every few minutes, a gardener would start a lawnmower. The beeping of the crosswalk was constant. Yan sat with his legs curled up. When I asked about his mother, he pondered for a moment. He told me she had a motto, a Chinese proverb: "Beyond the person, there are greater people; beyond the sky, there is more sky." She wasn't the kind of mother who would pressure her children, but she wanted him to know that no matter how good he thought he was, he only saw a small part of the world. She raised him and his sister alone in the heart of one of the most lucrative areas in American business history: Redwood Shores, between San Francisco and Palo Alto. Oracle's mirrored facade is located here, overlooking the community.
